title: Where Every S&P 500 Dollar Goes
summary: Where Every S&P 500 Dollar Goes 18 cents of every S&P 500 dollar now goes to semiconductors, 33 cents to the Mag 7, and 40 cents to the ten largest holdings. Passive money does not seek bargains, reinforcing concentration in the market’s largest positions. Image: Real Investment Advice

title: Seasonality – S&P 500 Index Average Monthly Returns
summary: Seasonality – S&P 500 Index Average Monthly Returns The S&P 500 is down about 3% this month, with the so-called June swoon back in play. Midterm years tend to amplify the softness. July, however, has a history of snapping the market back into gear. Image: Carson Investment Research

title: Cash Held by U.S. Households
summary: Cash Held by U.S. Households Equity inflows are drawing support from the cash buffers U.S. households amassed during the pandemic, with a large share of those savings yet to be deployed. Image: Deutsche Bank Asset Allocation

title: Hyperscaler Capex, EBITDA and Net Buybacks
summary: Hyperscaler Capex, EBITDA and Net Buybacks Hyperscaler capex is outpacing earnings and starting to bite. Buybacks are slipping as cash is redirected into AI infrastructure, setting up a tradeoff: more upside later, less cash in shareholders’ pockets today. Image: Deutsche Bank Asset Allocation

title: Gold Futures
summary: Gold Futures Gold’s 2026 gains have evaporated as sentiment flipped from rate cuts to higher-for-longer expectations. A stronger U.S. dollar is adding pressure, while forced selling to cover losses elsewhere is accelerating the selloff. Image: The Daily Shot

title: Probability of U.S. Recession Calculated from the Yield Curve
summary: Probability of U.S. Recession Calculated from the Yield Curve The probability of U.S. recession in 12 months, calculated from the yield curve, stands at 12.5%, keeping the expansion narrative intact. This cycle still has room to run. Image: Federal Reserve Bank of Cleveland

title: S&P 500 During The Dotcom Bubble

title: Who Gains in an AI-Supercharged Economy?
summary: If AI delivers as promised and fundamentally transforms the economy, we will have moved from the current investment phase, where AI builders dominate, to one where AI users will reap the greatest reward. That is what happens every time an innovation becomes a general-purpose technology.

title: Development in a Time of Disruption
summary: Once firms and economies ascend to market dominance, they often kick away the ladder behind them and erect barriers to market entry to stymie would-be challengers. But developing countries can and do leapfrog incumbent technologies, and conditions for doing so may be particularly favorable now.

title: Trump’s Un-American Capitalism
summary: The current US administration's openly corrupt style of crony capitalism is the antithesis of the institutional foundation on which the US economy was built. The winners in the new oligarchic competition are not those who make the best products, but rather those who are best at flattering the mad king.

title: Can the Manchester Model Save Britain?
summary: Following a decades-long effort to lay the foundations for economic dynamism, Greater Manchester has become the UK’s fastest-growing region. While the Manchester model cannot be precisely replicated at the national level, it does carry important lessons for outgoing Prime Minister Keir Starmer's likely successor.

title: How Trillionaires Are Really Made
summary: Some may argue that the world’s first trillionaire is a story of technological triumph and a win for American capitalism. But markets have not bet on cutting-edge technology or a company too big to fail, but rather on the idea of a mega-monopolist with wealth and influence so vast that the US government will not allow …
